nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, flooded forests, and tributaries; found among submerged leaf litter, roots, and woody debris; prefers murky, heavily structured environments with soft substrates

decorPreferences: caves and PVC pipes for daytime retreats
low-light or dim areas; floating plants to reduce overhead light
dense structure at tank edges and corners

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods; sinking wafers (algae wafers, catfish pellets), frozen bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, and bottom-dweller tablets are all taken readily. Scavenges leftover food from tankmates. Supplement with fresh vegetables (zucchini, cucumber) and meaty frozen foods to support long-term health. Feeds most actively after lights-out; target-feed nocturnally to ensure adequate intake.

goodWith: medium to large peaceful tetras (e.g., Congo tetra, bleeding heart tetra)
other armored catfish of similar size
avoidWith: small fish under 4 cm (may be accidentally consumed)
very small shrimp (predation risk at night)
aggressive fin-nippers that may harass a slow-moving catfish
predatory cichlids that can swallow medium fish
other Doradids in small tanks (resource competition)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Agamyxis pectinifrons; shows characteristic white-spotted black pattern on body and fins.

summary: A robust, armored catfish from the Amazon basin bearing a striking black body covered in irregular white spots. Hardy and long-lived (15–20 yr), peaceful toward similarly-sized tankmates, but pectoral spines lock erect and can snag nets or injure handlers. Strictly nocturnal; must be fed after lights-out.
careNotes:
The Spotted Raphael Catfish (Agamyxis pectinifrons) is one of the most durable and long-lived freshwater catfish available in the hobby, with well-kept specimens commonly reaching 15–20 years. Its bold white-spotted pattern on a jet-black body distinguishes it readily from the closely related Striped Raphael Catfish (Platydoras armatulus, fw-fish-233), which displays lateral white banding rather than scattered spots.

This species is a member of the family Doradidae — the "thorny" or "talking" catfishes — and shares two defining traits of the family: a row of lateral bony scutes along the flanks, and the ability to produce audible stridulatory sounds by rubbing the pectoral spine base against the pectoral girdle. The sound is most often heard when the fish is netted or stressed.

CRITICAL HANDLING NOTE: The pectoral fin spines lock rigidly erect when the fish is alarmed. They can pierce a net, tangle irreversibly, and puncture skin during handling. Always use a container or bag to transfer this species — never a net alone. Untangle cautiously; forcing a locked spine can injure the fish.

Being strictly nocturnal, the Spotted Raphael retreats into caves or dense cover during daylight hours and becomes active only after the tank lights dim. Target-feeding with sinking wafers or frozen foods placed near hides after lights-out ensures reliable feeding and prevents the fish from being outcompeted at the surface. A well-fitted tank lid is advisable (escape risk: moderate), as specimens occasionally climb along silicone seams or equipment tubes.

Amazon-appropriate water conditions (soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral, 22–28 °C) suit this species well, but it is fairly adaptable to neutral pH and moderate hardness. Weekly partial water changes and good biological filtration are important for long-term health; Doradids can carry external bacterial infections if kept in deteriorating water quality. Minimum tank size of 150 L (120 cm footprint) allows normal foraging behavior and adequate hiding territory.
breedingNotes: Not reported to breed in home aquaria. Wild spawning biology is poorly documented; members of Doradidae are presumed to be egg scatterers or substrate spawners, but reliable captive-breeding accounts for Agamyxis pectinifrons are absent from the hobby literature. Commercial stocks are wild-caught or bred in tropical fish farms in South America and Southeast Asia under conditions not replicated by hobbyists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Agamyxis pectinifrons
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Amazon basin: Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil), biotope, diet (omnivore, sinking foods), nocturnal behavior, pectoral spine locking hazard, stridulation, lifespan (15–20 yr), breeding (not bred in captivity), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Agamyxis pectinifrons
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Doradidae, Siluriformes), adult size (to 15.0 cm TL), native range (Amazon and Essequibo basins), temperature (22–28°C), and omnivore diet classification. Confirmed species as distinct from Platydoras armatulus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Talking Catfish / Raphael Catfish Care
additional.notes: Confirmed trade availability, peaceful temperament, nocturnal feeding requirement, pectoral spine locking (net hazard), stridulation, minimum tank size guidance (30–40 gal / ~115–150 L), and diet recommendations (sinking wafers, frozen foods). Hobbyist-oriented care guidance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Doradidae catfish overview
additional.notes: Supplementary source on family Doradidae; confirmed bony lateral scutes, stridulatory pectoral mechanism, typically nocturnal lifestyle, and long lifespan characteristic of the family. Corroborated spotted vs. banded pattern distinction between Agamyxis and Platydoras genera.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Platydoras armatulus (comparative reference fw-fish-233)
additional.notes: Used as comparative reference to distinguish Spotted Raphael (Agamyxis pectinifrons: scattered white spots on black) from Striped Raphael (Platydoras armatulus: white lateral bands on black). Confirms these are separate genera within Doradidae commonly confused in trade.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding records exist for this species. Commercially available specimens are wild-caught or farm-raised under undisclosed conditions not replicable by hobbyists.
